caveats: a: AI feasibility evidence comes mostly from broader manufacturing rather than LMM complete-aircraft plants.
a: Engineering intensity varies sharply between mature type designs and new or modified aircraft.
a: The range measures technical task exposure, not authorization to remove accountable personnel.
rho: Production-certificate and quality-system obligations limit black-box autonomy.
rho: Small OEMs may lack clean digital thread and labeled defect data.
rho: A severe quality event could cause adoption pauses or more human oversight.

e: A certified type design can have very low annual unit deliveries and highly volatile earnings.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may overstate acquisition-ready going concerns.
s5: FAA states that production certificates are not transferable, increasing transaction execution risk.
s5: ICON was a distressed asset transaction rather than a conventional going-concern sale.
s5: Pipistrel is a foreign precedent and was acquired by a large strategic buyer.
q: Government fixed-price programs can turn productivity gains into loss avoidance rather than incremental margin.
q: Customer deposits and delivery delays create cash-flow effects not captured by this retention share.
q: Competitive pricing differs substantially between certified proprietary aircraft and contract-built unmanned systems.
d5: Commercial-airline forecasts are not a direct demand forecast for LMM niche aircraft.
d5: Unit demand is sensitive to financing costs, government budgets, certification schedules, and export controls.
d5: Backlogs can contain options, deferrals, and orders with different cancellation protection.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Manufacturers earn milestone, progress, deposit, or delivery-based aircraft revenue under customer orders and government or commercial contracts, supplemented by options, spares, training, warranty, field support, and service. Certification and product configuration create long lead times; demand is lumpy by unit even where order books and installed-base support recur.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to independent LMM manufacturers of complete niche aircraft because industry output is dominated by a few global primes, while development-stage entrants, component suppliers, and MRO providers have fundamentally different certification, revenue, and availability economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Boeing, Lockheed Martin, General Dynamics, Textron, and other large commercial, business-jet, and defense primes; foreign-only OEMs; pre-revenue aircraft developers without repeat deliveries; component-only suppliers; MRO-only businesses; kit-only hobby operations; and businesses dependent on a single nonrecurring prototype contract.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers and prime integrators of complete light, utility, agricultural, training, unmanned, special-mission, and niche general-aviation aircraft with repeat production, certification, customer delivery, and product-support revenue.

a.bridge: NIST's cross-manufacturing evidence on predictive quality, maintenance, scrap, throughput, and planning, together with A3's industrial vision evidence, is mapped to the engineering, planning, quality, inspection, documentation, supply-chain, and assembly-support work of a niche aircraft OEM. FAA production-certificate requirements and aerospace quality oversight define the human-accountable process boundary. The estimate removes installed CAD/CAM and conventional automation and retains hands-on composite and metal work, systems installation, test, conformity, final assembly, and flight operations as largely non-addressable. Large, data-rich factory cases likely bias exposure upward; engineering-heavy LMM OEMs could have more document work than the base assumes.
caveats: AI feasibility evidence comes mostly from broader manufacturing rather than LMM complete-aircraft plants.
Engineering intensity varies sharply between mature type designs and new or modified aircraft.
The range measures technical task exposure, not authorization to remove accountable personnel.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-wage census from several certified niche US aircraft OEMs, separating current digital automation from unautomated engineering, quality, supply-chain, and production work, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can assist design search, engineering knowledge retrieval, configuration control, planning, supplier risk, visual and non-destructive-inspection triage, quality documentation, work instructions, maintenance analytics, and test-data review. Aircraft construction still depends on skilled physical assembly, inspection, conformity, testing, and sign-off, and stochastic tools cannot independently discharge airworthiness accountability.

d5.bridge: GAMA's 2025 worldwide shipment report for general-aviation and niche manufacturers and Boeing's long-term commercial-air-travel and fleet outlook are mapped to five-year real demand for US LMM light, utility, agricultural, training, unmanned, and special-mission aircraft. The base combines durable aviation growth, replacement, training, defense and public-safety demand, and visible small-OEM deliveries, but discounts the commercial-prime forecast for the target's different mix and high cyclicality. Boeing's large-jet outlook may bias the base upward for light aircraft, while defense UAS and special-mission demand could make it conservative.
caveats: Commercial-airline forecasts are not a direct demand forecast for LMM niche aircraft.
Unit demand is sensitive to financing costs, government budgets, certification schedules, and export controls.
Backlogs can contain options, deferrals, and orders with different cancellation protection.
d5.change_evidence: A bottom-up five-year order, cancellation, replacement, and funding forecast for eligible US niche OEMs by aircraft mission, backed by customer deposits and program appropriations, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Aviation demand benefits from fleet replacement, travel and cargo growth, training, agriculture, defense, and special missions. GAMA's 2025 report documents active deliveries across small manufacturers as well as large ones, and Boeing projects sustained long-run fleet growth. Five-year outcomes remain lumpy because certification delays, financing rates, export policy, and a handful of contracts can dominate an LMM OEM.

caveats: GAMA reporting is global and voluntary and does not map directly to US NAICS firms.
A certified type design can have very low annual unit deliveries and highly volatile earnings.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may overstate acquisition-ready going concerns.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of US aircraft manufacturers linked to ownership, revenue, EBITDA, certification status, delivery history, product-support revenue, and dependence on nonrecurring development contracts would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The eligible niche-OEM population is real—GAMA reports deliveries by small light-aircraft, agricultural-aircraft, and helicopter manufacturers—but the industry's output and employment are dominated by very large commercial, business-jet, and defense primes. The broad code also includes development-stage entrants, kit makers, and entities whose economics do not meet the recurring-production LMM lens.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $27,080.8M / sectoral output (T30) $100,668.3M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $542,849 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336411); class <5: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $605,836 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $54,768 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,195,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $198,443 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,575,059 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $413,585 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,530,820 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,132,786 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $54,507,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,927,499 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,228,711,862 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $291,875,552 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 83

caveats: This primitive excludes ordinary cycle and order volatility represented in d5.
A single-model OEM can face near-total displacement if a replacement program fails.
Autonomy can expand aircraft demand in some missions even as it reduces crewed-aircraft demand in others.
o.change_evidence: Mission-level procurement and fleet plans quantifying substitution by satellites, ground systems, remotely piloted platforms, fleet sharing, foreign sourcing, and platform consolidation would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Complete aircraft deliver physical mobility, mission, training, agricultural, and defense capabilities that software cannot replace. The relevant outside threats are mission substitution by satellites or ground systems, shared or remotely piloted fleets reducing unit requirements, foreign competition, customer insourcing, program consolidation, and new aircraft architectures displacing incumbent models. These risks matter to individual OEMs but are unlikely to remove much aggregate five-year need.

caveats: Government fixed-price programs can turn productivity gains into loss avoidance rather than incremental margin.
Customer deposits and delivery delays create cash-flow effects not captured by this retention share.
Competitive pricing differs substantially between certified proprietary aircraft and contract-built unmanned systems.
q.change_evidence: Comparable niche-OEM contracts, bid histories, gross-margin bridges, customer discounting, and observed repricing after manufacturing improvements would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Aircraft are differentiated, certified products sold through negotiated unit, option, government, or fleet contracts rather than automotive-style annual productivity price-downs. An OEM can retain internal engineering, rework, schedule, and quality savings between bids, but sophisticated buyers negotiate discounts, fixed-price government work transfers cost risk, and competitive models constrain future pricing. General Dynamics' business-jet accounting confirms delivery-and-acceptance economics, while no representative evidence directly isolates AI savings retention at LMM OEMs.

rho.bridge: Cross-manufacturing AI deployments are translated into five-year realization for niche aircraft OEMs and discounted using FAA evidence that production approval depends on audited organizations, facilities, quality systems, design data, and continuing compliance. Uses that advise planning, retrieval, inspection triage, and maintenance can deploy earlier; uses that alter approved design, process control, conformity, or release decisions require validation, traceability, cybersecurity, and human approval. Generic manufacturing cases likely bias realization upward, while low-code document and analytics tools could make the low case conservative.
caveats: Production-certificate and quality-system obligations limit black-box autonomy.
Small OEMs may lack clean digital thread and labeled defect data.
A severe quality event could cause adoption pauses or more human oversight.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at FAA production-certificate holders showing approval path, validation time, data lineage, error rates, labor savings, rework reduction, and scaled use across aircraft configurations would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Certification and safety culture slow production-system change, and FAA oversight demonstrates that quality failures can constrain output. Realization is most credible in assistive workflows that preserve engineering and quality sign-off. Gains should appear in fewer errors, rework, schedule slips, and engineering hours before they appear as direct labor removal.

s5.bridge: ICON's 2024 transfer of substantially all assets to SG Investment America and Textron's 2022 acquisition of light-aircraft manufacturer Pipistrel are observed transfers of niche aircraft OEM capability, mapped to the probability that an eligible US LMM OEM changes control within five years. ICON was an asset sale following Chapter 11 and Pipistrel was foreign and acquired by a much larger strategic buyer, so the base is discounted for distress, geography, and buyer-scale mismatch. Including distress may overstate willing availability but also demonstrates that type designs and installed-base support can transfer when the operating company struggles.
caveats: FAA states that production certificates are not transferable, increasing transaction execution risk.
ICON was a distressed asset transaction rather than a conventional going-concern sale.
Pipistrel is a foreign precedent and was acquired by a large strategic buyer.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year denominator of US independent niche aircraft OEMs matched to control sales, asset sales, restructurings, closures, and failed marketing processes would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Certified designs, production know-how, brands, order books, and installed-base support attract strategic capital. Availability remains constrained by founder attachment, nontransferable FAA production certificates, development liabilities, warranty and accident exposure, customer deposits, working-capital needs, and a narrow buyer universe capable of sustaining certification and support.

principal_driver: Engineering- and quality-intensive workflows create meaningful AI exposure inside a product category with durable replacement and mission demand and relatively differentiated pricing.
principal_weakness: The LMM target universe is thin, and FAA certification, nontransferable production approvals, safety accountability, and lumpy single-model demand make both AI realization and acquisition execution unusually fragile.
